sentences.text: Sure.
I guess the most essential things.
We-- I, I do know that my-- some stuff that was still saved even from the apartment, you know, when we were bombed, a few pieces that somehow survived.
I remember going up there.
There were no steps.
And there were just some boards.
And, and we went up there to see what, what was there.
And a few pieces, my parents were able to save.
And they gave it to some Gentile friends of ours with the idea that if we-- you know, someday they would give it back to us.
And some of the stuff that we still had was given to friends.
And we just took the most essential things--
Well, clothing-- and I, I do know that a fur-- my mother's fur coat.
And just before the war, again, because my father such was a pessimist, he wanted to invest the money.
I even, I was only 15.
I got a fur coat, an ocelot coat.
It looked like a tiger coat.
But anyway, so I got that fur coat, too.
And that we took in, took with us.
And just clothing, I think, mostly and maybe a few parts.
I don't-- I don't really remember.
But as I say, we just had one room.
So we couldn't-- i didn't-- we didn't even have a closet there because it was a dining room.
So we stayed there for a while.
Also, we, we established a library in this apartment building.
And young people would man the library.
And we would lend the books.
Naturally, nobody had a radio.
At least there were some radios.
But they were like in the underground.
They, they were, were forbidden.
I mean, the radios were taken away.
And matter of fact, skis were taken away very early.
I think they were already preparing for the invasion even before the ghetto, invasion of Norway.
And people had to bring in skis.
And so I-- so we had this, we had this little library.
And in some apartment building, the young people got together.
And they were putting on shows, you know, and just to keep the spirit up.
But we didn't stay there for long.
Because this part of the ghetto, this street, was cut off the ghetto.
It was too good.
So we had to move.
And we moved to another apartment on [INAUDIBLE] Street.
And there we-- again, we had a room.
It wasn't a bedroom.
I, I mean, it wasn't a dining room.
So it had a door.
And I know that the other people who lived in this room, one woman was a gynecologist, obstetrician-gynecologist.
And she was there by herself.
Then there was another woman whose husband apparently was-- got killed in the beginning.
And he was a lawyer.
She was there with her eight-year-old daughter.
And one woman who was a refugee from Lodz-- I mean, a couple from Lodz and this woman was-- spoke French beautifully.
And she taught-- give, give me lessons in, in French.
Well, people were starting to die of typhus and starvation.
We all had ration cards.
But--
I don't remember.
All I know is that there were ration cards.
But I also know that there were some people that were so poor, apparently, that couldn't afford the ration cards.
You had to pay for them.
There was a tax on it.
And some people were so, so poor that they couldn't even afford the-- the price of the ration card.
And they would sell it and just-- depends on the soup kitchen.
Because there was, as I mentioned, soup kitchen established.
But people were just dying in, in, in the street.
The most horrid [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things were the, the people who-- children, you know, lying in the, in the street, swollen from hunger.
And some, some kids were just going, and especially towards the evening, and, and yelling, throw down a piece bread, a piece of moldy bread, you know, in, in Yiddish.
And it was just heart breaking.
And you couldn't do.
You could give them-- maybe if you had a piece of bread, you could give them a, a piece of bread.
And so, so there were-- especially the people who were refugees from the surrounding towns who were poor to start with and couldn't help themselves.
But there was a, a quite a robust economy going on in the ghetto.
It was like an underground economy.
People were making all kind-- well, first of all, there were some factories established.
We called them shops.
And some of them were run by Germans.
And some of them were run by Jews, really, who had some-- not connection.
But the German was supervising or somehow, they were all going for Germany.
But there was the brush makers and the all kinds of stuff.
They were making utensils out of metal that was smuggled in from some downed airplanes or so.
And they were making spoons and forks.
And, and that was being exported to the Aryan side of the ghetto.
And then I remember, they were making .
Boots we all had boots.
The, the sole was wood.
And the upper part was some kind of a felt that I think was made out of old newspapers or something or cardboard.
And, and somehow, it was treated chemically.
So they were making boots.
They were making all kinds of scarves out of dying-- you know, sheets that were being dyed different colors and then [INAUDIBLE].
Most of the peasant women wore these babushkas.
So there were these babushkas.
And there was a, a almost a thriving economy.
And this was being smuggled out from the ghetto.
And I assume most of them was smuggled out with the knowledge of the Germans because-- with bribery and, and whatnot.
And so some-- and exchange, again, for food.

And I guess you heard about these children that were going through the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over the, the brick wall, or rather, under the, the wall with digging holes and getting out of the ghetto and bringing in food, smuggling in food.
So I, I was caught.
The Germans could come in.
And also, there were labor battalions that were coming out of the ghetto.
They could come to-- going to work.
The Germans also, when they needed some special work or something, they would come in and catch people in the street.
And I was caught once.
I mean, I was caught several times.
But once, the worst experience I had was when they took me to some kind of a big building, well, like barracks for soldiers.
But it was a like big building.
I don't know if it was a old school or something.
And they gave me a pail of water.
And that was in the winter.
They gave me some cold water and they told me to wash the windows and-- outside and inside.
And frankly, I had never washed windows in my life, and certainly not with cold water.
And I-- there was this one Germany.
He was probably a [NON-ENGLISH].
He knew some Polish.
And I asked him in Polish.
Because at that time, we were told don't show that you know the German language.
Because they might-- there were rumors that they will take, you know, young girls to some brothers for-- not so much for German soldiers.
I think it was more for the Lithuanian or Ukrainian soldiers.
I don't know if it was [NON-ENGLISH] or not for the Germans.
But anyway, so that-- there were the rumors.
So don't, don't speak any German, and so.
So I ask in Polish for a rag.
And they said, no.
I need a rag, I should, I should use my blouse and-- which I wouldn't do.
So I was sitting on that windowsill with a bucket of water and spraying the water on top of, of the windowpane and scraping it with my fingernails.
And I didn't-- you know, I was balancing myself on that window pane, on that windowsill.
And I wore a skirt.
We didn't wear slacks like we do now.
So my skirt would-- when I was reaching, my skirt would slide up.
And there was these soldiers, young soldiers.
And they would make remarks or look.
And I didn't know if I should pull my skirt down or if I should hold on to the bucket, if I should, you know?
And that was going on for quite a while.
And then finally, I thought that my hands would fall off because they were so frozen.
Because the water would immediately freeze, it was so cold.
So then I started to hum like a little German song that I knew.
And the soldiers realized, you know, that I knew some German.
So I said, I don't care what happens.
And so I started to speak German with them.
So they told me to come down and just to-- gave me a rag and just to wash the insides of the, of the windows.
And after that, I, I got the rye bread, like a two kilos.
And that was the first real good rye bread that I've seen in a long time.
Because to get-- the bread that we got in, in the ghetto was black, you know, very dark and full of potato peels and pebbles.
And everything was put in there to make it heavier.
It was adulterated with all kinds of junk.
And you couldn't even slice it.
When you tried to slice it, it would just kind of fall apart.
And so anyway, I had this rye bread.
And I tried to bring it back into the ghetto.
And I was-- t German took it from me at the gate and threw it in the-- on the ground and said, that's too good for, for Jews, and just stamped on it.
So I didn't-- and I was very hungry that day.
Because, I was caught in the morning.
But as-- in spite of all that, there was a cultural life going on.
There were concerts.
There were many musicians who found themselves in the ghetto.
And they organized a symphony.
And so we had classical music.
And then concerts were given.
And my love for classical music actually developed in the ghetto.
Now, the funny thing is that I remember Beethoven and Mozart being played.
And I have read in some book that, that the Germans forbade any composers except for Jewish composers, which I didn't even know.
Because all I know is that I, I fell in love with Mozart.
And I met-- about a year ago, I met a man who had survived.
And he was a musician in, in the ghetto.
He was a violinist-- a pianist.
I ask him.
I said, do you remember, you know?
And he apparently played in, in that symphony.
So I said, I can't understand why they were saying that only-- no German composer, that only Jewish composers would be played.
And he said, oh, sure.
That was the official line.
And we would put these ad-- not ads, but announcements-- Mendelssohn, always Mendelssohn.
But they-- once you were in, they played whatever they wanted to play.
But officially, it was always Mendelssohn.
And then there were these clandestine courses organized for children-- for young people.
Classes?
Yes, classes, because there were so many [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] teachers that found themselves, and high school teachers and even professors, university professors.
So they organized these clandestine courses.
And I attended one of them.
There were about six girls-- five, six girls.
And we would meet with a different teacher every day.
And then, you know, have a lot of homework.
Well, actually, I got a very good education.
I was taught Latin by somebody, by a known classicists.
I don't remember her name.
I was taught physics and calculus by a-- from a astronomer, a older man.
And I remember him sitting-- he had-- you know, in the winter, we only had this one little [NON-ENGLISH], little lamp.
That was the only source of heat and, and light.
And I remember him with gloves on, except for the finger, so he could write.
And we were all huddled around this lamp.
And he was explaining some formulas and this.
I still have that picture in my mind, you know, this older professor there.
Of
Yeah.
We had some text books.
But we couldn't carry them.
We couldn't enter an apartment all together.
We just entered it singly, like every five minutes.
And we always had some game, like the Polish version of Monopoly, something like that, or dominoes or cards prepared.
So in case somebody walks in, we would just pretend we had a social gathering.
Because it was forbidden to, to study.
And so I had Latin.
And I had French by a woman who had studied at the Sorbonne.
And she-- so we had French.
And I-- and then we had history with one professor.
I remember his name was Brahms.
And I remember-- I think somebody was teaching Polish, Polish literature.
But I do remember studying-- mostly studying, you know, my calculus.
I had the most problem with that.
We also would meet and read books.
And that was even a, a different group of kids.
And we would read philosophy.
I was very interested in philosophy.
And then we would discuss, you know, things.
I remember, we read Aldous Huxley's And then we would discuss it for hours, if it would be a good world or if he really meant it, or if it was a satire, or what it was.
So we-- one boy found a little attic room that wasn't occupied.
And he did like a window up on, on the ceiling and so we could look out at the beautiful blue sky.
That was in '42.
And we would-- and he wrote poetry.
So we were-- so we would meet there.
And he would read poetry.

And we just did all these things just to hold on to our humanity, you know, not to be devoured by, by what was going on around us, although we did lose one-- one friend of mine go typhus and, and died.
So, so there were these two parts of life in the ghetto.
There was this horrid, constant fear and hunger.
And on the other hand, there was this attempt at culture, life.
And-- Well, let's, let's talk a little bit more about food.
Oh, I, I, I don't know how my mother got food.
But mostly what we had was the so-called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was one part-- you know, a heavy soup.
So she would get somewhere potatoes and some carrots, and, and whatever, some vegetables, and make that soup and bread.
And so we were always hungry, you know?
But as I mentioned before, my parents had these, these few gold pieces.
And so for money, you, you could get some food.
There wasn't-- you couldn't get any steak or filet mignon.
But you could-- once in a while, you got-- on the ratio cards, you got, I don't know, one egg a week or something.
So the eggs were there.
So if you had some money, you could get-- buy some more eggs, for instance.
And-- but mostly, I think it was bread and jam that we were getting on, on ration cards.
So-- then the, the-- it start-- then in 1942, the great deportations started.
And about eight to 10 people-- 8,000 to 10,000 people a day were, were deported.
And at that time, we started to hear rumors about people being killed on the east.
Naturally, the deportation started as resettlement.
That's what the Germans would say.
I want to mention that we also discussed frequently the idea of armed resistance or some kind of resistance.
But it was frowned upon.
Because we were so sure that it was just a matter of time.
We were sure that the Allied would win.
And that it was just a matter of staying there and waiting, you know?
And if you didn't die of hunger or of typhus, you survive.
We didn't know about the mass killings.
So when-- so-- and, and there were always hostages taken.
If you injured a German or killed a German, there were, you know, so many people-- dozens of people taken as hostages.
And some were never heard from again.
They were either shot immediately or sent out somewhere.
So we felt that we had no right to jeopardize the lives of innocent people just because we want to take revenge.
Naturally, that feeling had changed.
And towards the end of 1942, when, when the young people started to arm themselves.
And that culminated in the ghetto uprising in '43, which-- Were you aware of this group?
No.
I wasn't aware of this group.
But I wasn't aware of the change in atmosphere of more talk about resistance.
You mean the Judenrat?
Yeah, well, we all knew about the Judenrat.
And I, I don't know.
We hear all kinds of things.
Some people are-- that said, you know, criticized the Judenrat.
And some people felt-- feel a lot of hostility.
But I think they just had to do their best.
I mean, there was-- had to be some kind of an organization or government who was coordinating things.
And I think the Judenrat tried as best as it could.
But--
No, not personally, no.
Well, that's-- you know, we hated the police, not so much in the beginning when they were just police-- traffic and for minor infractions.
But towards the end, like in 1942, when they were taking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 6,000, 8,000, and 10,000 people a day.
And so they were cooperating with the German, trying to catch these people or coming and blocking off-- I mean, not the Jewish police.
Germans would, would block off a street.
And all that people had come down from the apartment.
And then they would check the cards, work cards.
At that time, you could only stay in the ghetto if you worked for the German-- for one of those factories.
And-- or for the Judenrat of for the police.
And, and if you didn't, and if you caught, you were sent, sent away.
I-- we had to move again.
I mean, from [INAUDIBLE] we moved to [INAUDIBLE].
And then we moved to another place.
And we worked for, worked for [?
Schultz, ?] which was-- if you know it was like a factory like Schindler, only the man who owned it was a German.
But he wasn't, wasn't as, as good as Schindler.
So that was factory with furs.
They were making fur jackets and stuff like that for the army on the Russian front.
And so we were able to work for [?
Schultz. ?]
Now, the ghetto was divided into self-contained compounds.
It was like labor camp, actually.
Because we, we worked in this factory.
And then right across the street, we had the building where the people who worked there lived.
Yes.
My father worked there.
And I worked there.
My mother worked in a different factory.
My mother worked sewing.
I got the-- my father worked in the office.
And I worked also in the office.
Now, my father did all kinds of accounting.
And what I know is that this Mr.
Schultz ?] was cheating the government, you know?
Because they were paying him so much for it.
And what he really wanted from my father is to cook the books, to make, make it so that, that he could cheat.
And I guess my father knew how to do it.
So he was, he was always good in accounting.
And so my-- and I, once I asked my father, you know, how he felt about it.
He says, well, if it-- if the government or the army or who had to pay more, it was fine with him.
He didn't care if [?
Schultz ?] got the rich or not.
But, but it was a pretty, you know, safe job.
But even then, at one time-- I remember in September, we were all take to a, a small area.
And all the documents were checked.
And it was a horrid, horrid experience.
They were selecting, you know, very slowly.
And I remember we were in a kind of a burnt out house.
We had to spend, like, two nights, I think.
And my father just got gray.
And what my mother got some soot from a old stove or something.
And then she put the soot on his hair so to make, make it dark, you know, so he wouldn't show that he was a older man.
And she also found a piece--
Yeah, well, the older people were taken.
And so she always found some paper from chicory.
You know what chicory is, when you-- and she, and she put the chicory on his, on his cheeks so to give him color, you know, that papers.
Because we didn't have rouge or anything.
So it was the chicory that gave him healthy-looking cheeks and, and the soot that gave him black hair.
Yes.
But well, the idea was not to.
But if somebody looked old and sick, they would take him.
So--
Sure.
